coredata.dc:title: High added value functionalized carbon quantum dots synthetized from orange peels by assisted microwave solvothermal method and their performance as photosensitizer of mesoporous TiO2 photoelectrodes
coredata.dc:creator: Olmos-Moya, Patricia M.
Velazquez-Martinez, Sergio
Pineda-Arellano, Carlos
Rangel-Mendez, J. Rene
Chazaro-Ruiz, Luis F.
coredata.dc:description: A direct pathway to convert orange peels waste to useful co-functionalized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was performed through a friendly and efficient “greener method” consisting of a one-step microwave assisted solvothermal synthesis. The reaction proceeds in a mixture of powder of orange peels as precursor and water. The biomass was characterized by ICP-OES and CHONS analysis together with Fiber's analysis and FT-IR spectroscopy. The prepared CQDs presented a photoluminescent of brightness blue, high aqueous dispersion, chemical stability, an average particle size of 1.16 nm ± 0.1 nm and surface carboxylic and amine groups together with some dopants from the bio-precursor. The Fluorescence study revealed their potential emission in the electromagnetic spectrum of visible light. The bare mesoporous TiO2 semiconductor was impregnated with CQDs and the resultant composites presented a red-shift in the energy band gap with values from 2.3 eV to 2.08 eV, clearly lower than those on bare semiconductor and depending on the amount of CQDs. The CQDs/TiO2 photoelectrodes increased their photocurrent when they were exposed to the UV–Vis light, a decrease of photoelectrochemical band gap energy and an increase of the charge transfer constant in contrast with bare TiO2. These CQDs/TiO2 photoanodes were evaluated in carbon quantum dots-sensitized solar cells (CQDSSC) where there was an increase in the performance of the photovoltaic parameters compared with the bare semiconductor.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A direct pathway to convert orange peels waste to useful co-functionalized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was performed through a friendly and efficient “greener method” consisting of a one-step microwave assisted solvothermal synthesis. The reaction proceeds in a mixture of powder of orange peels as precursor and water. The biomass was characterized by ICP-OES and CHONS analysis together with Fiber's analysis and FT-IR spectroscopy. The prepared CQDs presented a photoluminescent of brightness blue, high aqueous dispersion, chemical stability, an average particle size of 1.16 nm ± 0.1 nm and surface carboxylic and amine groups together with some dopants from the bio-precursor. The Fluorescence study revealed their potential emission in the electromagnetic spectrum of visible light. The bare mesoporous TiO2 semiconductor was impregnated with CQDs and the resultant composites presented a red-shift in the energy band gap with values from 2.3 eV to 2.08 eV, clearly lower than those on bare semiconductor and depending on the amount of CQDs. The CQDs/TiO2 photoelectrodes increased their photocurrent when they were exposed to the UV–Vis light, a decrease of photoelectrochemical band gap energy and an increase of the charge transfer constant in contrast with bare TiO2. These CQDs/TiO2 photoanodes were evaluated in carbon quantum dots-sensitized solar cells (CQDSSC) where there was an increase in the performance of the photovoltaic parameters compared with the bare semiconductor.

ce:section.ce:para: Demand for renewable energy, clean energy resources, such as solar is increasing, as a result of emissions and severe environmental pollution caused by the combustion of traditional unsustainable fossil resources [1–3]. Therefore, current studies have focused on the development of effective and reliable energy conversion and storage technologies [4–6]. Solar technology, including solar cells, is the only way to achieve high solar-to-electric energy conversation efficiency and it can be quickly configured on a large scale. In the effort to respond to the latter concerns, third generation solar cells are an alternative, which can be classified as dye-sensitized cells, organic cells, perovskite cells, and quantum dot cells, where the name of each type of solar cell describes the principal nature of the light absorbing material that forms the active layer [7,8].

In particular, with respect to quantum dot-sensitized solar cells (QDSSC), various types of colloidal inorganic quantum dots (QDs) such as PbS, CdS, CdSe have been used as adsorbers-emitters for solar cells and for certain coupled devices as luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs)-photovoltaic cells, due to their tunable absorption/emission spectra, high quantum yield (QY), high absorption coefficient, size/shape/composition, and good photostability [9–11], unfortunately, these QDs contain toxic and expensive elements. However, in recent years the colloidal carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted much attention for application in sensitized solar cells due to their absorption coefficient comparable to inorganic QDs [12–14], size-tunable absorption, high QY, low cost, and eco-friendly [10,15].

The concept of using CQDs as sensitizer of mesoscopic TiO2 solar cells was first reported by Yan et al., in 2010 they used graphene as precursor and achieved a power conversion efficiency of 0.056% [16]. Following, Mirtchev et al., reported a nanocrystalline TiO2-based solar cell sensitized whit CQDs prepared by means of a butyroacetone carbonization synthesis, obtaining an efficiency of 0.13% [17]. Since then, this topic has been further researched for practical applications.

However, reducing the cost of producing CQDs on a large scale remains a challenge. Therefore, during this development progress, enormous efforts have been made to develop green methodologies for preparing CQDs using natural substances widely distributed in nature, such as carbohydrates including glucose, sucrose, fructose [18]. In this regard, only some works have reported the preparation of CQDs through a green synthesis method from algal blooms [19], lignin [20], grape juice [21], banana juice [22], sugarcane molasses [23]; mainly by hydrothermal approaches. Also, orange peels [24] and quince fruit [25] have been used as carbon source to prepare CQDs via microwave assisted methods. Depending on the bioprecursor and synthesis method, CQDs will contain main features such as a core with a specific crystalline structure and certain functional groups on the surface which may improve their optical and electrochemical properties [26].

CQDS obtained from biomass have shown good characteristics for their practical application in the manufacture of third generation photovoltaic cells sensitized with CQDs prepared from organic waste. This, with the aim of obtaining cleaner and cheaper solar cells. For example, more recently Briscoe et al., reported the performance of solar cells sensitized with biomass-derived CQDs that acted as a coating on a ZnO semiconductor. The cells presented a PCE of 0.077% [27]. Also, Meng et al., reported the use of a renewable agro-food precursor, to prepare CQDs that were used to sensitize mesoscopic TiO2 combined with green-light long persistence phosphors (LPPs). Their biomass-derived QDSSC yielded a PCE of 0.104% [28]. In the same way, Yang et al., obtained CQDs from a biomass via simple one-pot method and they explored their application as sensitizers for TiO2-based nanostructured solar cells. They proposed that the presence of functional groups on the surface of CQDs favored the increase of PEC and found a value of 0.17% [29]. These authors affirmed that the exploration of potential biomass material to produce photovoltaic cells sensitized with CQDs prepared from organic waste is still necessary.

In this work it is reported the preparation of CQDs from orange peels since this agro-industrial waste play the role of an excellent natural source of carbon and heteroatoms for the synthesis of CQDs with graphitic core, certain doping with heteroatoms as N, Si, P, B, and co-functionalized with carboxyl and amino groups due to the presence of multicomponents: soluble compounds such as glucose, fructose, citric acid, phenols, flavonoids, carotenoids and minerals [30,31]. In this study, it was implemented a synthesis method that would meet the green synthesis criteria, friendly to the environment, without the use of acid-base compounds, oxidants, or organic solvents before, during or after the synthesis process as previously reported [32]. The preparation of CQDs through protocols of microwave assisted synthesis has many advantages such as improving reproducibility via temperature, pressure and microwave power control, stirring of the solution, homogeneous microwave irradiation power on the reaction medium, a higher safety, as well as convenient programing and data management [33,34]. This allowed to obtain CQDs with good reproducibility in a short time. Furthermore, during the microwave assisted synthesis different reaction parameters were evaluated, particularly the microwave irradiation power and temperature. The CQDs were used as photosensitizers of TiO2. Finally, “CQDs/TiO2” photoanodes of thin film were manufactured to evaluate the correlation between the spectroscopic and photoelectrochemical performance of the hybrid materials, which was made to the study of the influence of these properties on the tuning levels and band gap energy change. It is worth mentioning that it is the first time that the use of CQDs synthesized from orange peel powder is reported as photosensitizers of TiO2 semiconductor for the preparation of photoanodes with possible application in carbon quantum dots-sensitized solar cells (CQDSSC).

Hydrochloric acid, sodium hydroxide, sodium chloride, and nitric acid, potassium ferricyanide, potassium chloride were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Orange fruit was purchased from a local market during 2019 and washed thoroughly with tap and distilled water. Neutral Detergent solution, acid detergent solution and alpha-amylase solutions were purchase from ANKOM Technology. Tubes with filter units for centrifuge with 3KD permittivity were purchased from Merck Millipore. All synthesis and characterization, when needed, used deionized water (18 MΩ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs using orange peels as bio-precursor
ce:section.ce:para: In a glassy microwave vessel, 0.1 g of powder of orange peels were suspended in 20 mL of deionized water. The vessel was tightly sealed, place in microwave oven, and the reaction temperature was risen to 220 °C in 1 min and 48 s approximately using 850 W microwave irradiations. Reaction proceeded at this temperature for 30 min with a control of microwave irradiation at 700 W. After cooling, the brown solution was centrifuged to separate large particles. Finally, the brown solution was purified using 3KD centrifugal filter units. The particles were dried and concentrated, and the resulting powder was used for further studies. The variation of some parameters of the synthesis process such as the reaction temperature and the power of microwave irradiation was carried out in order to evaluate their influence on the mechanism of formation of the CQDs: 180 °C and 600 W, 200 °C and 650 W for a reaction time of 30 min.

The HRTEM study was performed with JEOL- 200 CX electron microscope for a sample of scattered CQDs on carbon-coated cooper grid at 80 KV. A drop of residual CQDs after drying and saturation was placed on glassy substrate for Raman spectroscopy analysis using the InVia Microraman: Renishaw Spectrometer equipped with a wavelength laser of 532 nm and 633 nm, Notch holographic filters and scattered light photodetector. The UV–Visible absorption spectra were recorded on an Agilent Cary 6000i UV–vis–NIR spectrophotometer, equipped with an integrating sphere using 1 mL CQDs solutions in deionized water, which were prepared with a dissolution factor of 1:7 (v/v) contained in a quartz cell. The FT-IR study was carried out with an ISASA-FTIR spectrometer coupled to a microscope with a frequency range between 4000 and 400 cm−1. The sample of CQDs was placed evenly on the gold plate for FTIR analysis. Distribution of particle size was determined through zeta-potential measurements using an aqueous suspension of CQDs (4-fold dilution). The measurements were performed by static light scattering using a Nanoparticles Size Analyzer NANOTRAC WAVE II instrument. For photoluminescence analysis a drop of CQDs sample was placed on the slides which were observed under an epifluorescence Zeiss microscope Axio Imager M2 though the AxioCam MRm camera and Zen 2012 (blue edition) software. Different filter sets were tested, like filter set 49 with blue emission (ʎEx 365 - ʎEm 445/50), filter set 38 with green emission (ʎEx 470/40 - ʎEm 525/50), filter set 00 with red emission (ʎEx 530/585 - ʎEm 615).

The titrations curves were determined with a Mettler Toledo - Titration Excellence T70. 150 mg of CQDs were added to a bottle containing 20 mL solution of 0.1 M NaCl as electrolyte. These bottles were agitated for 24 h and then adjusted to pH 11 with 0.1 M NaOH. Consecutively the proton binding curves and pKa distribution plot for CQDs sample was obtained using the software SAEIUS-pK-Dist [35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of CQDs/TiO2 composites
ce:section.ce:para: A paste of TiO2 nanoparticles was prepared by the sol-gel method. First, a mixture of deionized water and ethanol was prepared in a vol/vol ratio (1:12) respectively under stirring and immediately hydrochloric acid was added. This solution was added dropwise to a flask with 20.7 mL of titanium isopropoxide solution, maintaining constant stirring for 24 h. After this time a solvothermal treatment was applied at 200 °C for 12 h. The resulting nanoparticles/solvent suspension was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 20 min. Finally, the obtained TiO2 nanoparticles were rinsed with anhydrous ethanol and recentrifuged at 7000 rpm, and this process was repeated twice. The procedure to prepare the composites consisted of preparing suspensions of TiO2 nanoparticles together with CQDs in deionized water with two dilution factors of CQDs to obtain three suspensions with different concentrations of CQDs. The three resulting suspensions were prepared in 1:1, 1:2 and 1:3 ratios of TiO2:CQDs, which were stirring at a constant temperature of 35 °C during 12 h.

The diffuse reflectance of the TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 samples was measured using an Agilent Cary 6000i UV–Vis–NIR spectrophotometer equipped with a diffuse reflectance attachment. The incident beam was collimated, and reflected light was captured by an integrating sphere. Surface morphology, internal structure, and elemental analysis (EDS) were carried out using a dual beam scanning electron microscopy (FIB/SEM) (FEI-Helios Nanolab 600). The identification of the crystalline phases of TiO2 was performed from the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns obtained in a SmartLab RIGAKU diffractometer with CuK radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å). The scanning range was from 20° to 80° for TiO2–C. The scanning speed was 5° min−1 with a scanning step of 0.01°. The scanning speed was 5° min−1 with a scanning step of 0.01°.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of CQDs-sensitized photoanodes and photoelectrochemical studies
ce:section.ce:para: The photoanodes were prepared through the screen-printing method, using 2.0 × 2.0 cm cut FTO (SnO2:F)-glasses substrates as photocurrent collectors impregnated with a paste of TiO2 nanoparticles. The TiO2 electrodes were immersed in a solution containing 2.8 mg mL−1 of CQDs for 24 h to obtain the sensitized TiO2 photoanodes. The area of the resulting photoanode was 0.5 cm × 1.0 cm, which was submitted to heat treatment at 530 °C for 1 h.

The electrochemical and photoelectrochemical measurements were performed with a potentiostat/galvanostat model VSP SAS Bio-Logic controlled by EC-Lab software V 10.23 using a conventional three-electrode cell. A CQDs/TiO2 photoanode as working electrode, Pt mesh as counter electrode and Ag/AgCl/NaCl 3 M as reference electrode (RE). The sensitized photoanodes were appropriately positioned towards the side of the cell on which the light irradiation was total. The illumination was done with a simulator system with arrangement of the UV and visible light lamps of direct irradiation with an emission of 365 nm and from 365 to 850 nm. All cyclic voltammetry tests were performed in 0.1 M KCl at a potential scan rate of 25 mV s−1. The scan started at the open circuit potential in a cathodic direction until a reversal potential of Eλ = −0.8 V, then in anodic direction until a reversal potential of Eλ = 1.2 V vs. Ag/AgCl/NaCl 3 M. Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy was carrier out to 0.140 V (vs. RE), frequency range: from 20 kHz to 1 mHz, width ca: 10 mV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of CQDs/TiO2 photoanodes in CQDSSC
ce:section.ce:para: For the assembly of the carbon quantum dots-sensitized solar cells (CQDSSC) (Fig. S2.7), a Surlyn 1720 polymer frame was used between the working and the counter electrodes which was previously coated with a platinum film. The whole assembly was then held together, and the electrolyte based on the I−/I3 pair (lodolyte HI-30 from Solaronix) was injected and then a silver contact was added. To obtain the I–V curves a SciSun-300 solar simulator of the SCIENCETECH brand of Xenon short-arc lamp UXL-150S0 was used in conditions of air mass AM 1.5 (1000 W/m2) in conjunction with a source-Keithley Model 2400 Voltage-Current Meter to get the current and voltage data.

The CQDs with highly water solubility were facilely synthesized by a simple microwave assisted hydrothermal process, from orange peels as raw materials (illustrated in Fig. 1 ). The CQDs mechanism of formation starts when the primary components (Tables S3.1 and S3.2) of powder orange peel such cellulose, hemicellulose and crude proteins were hydrolyzed into glucose and amino acids, which are integrated with the other soluble compounds such as fructose, ascorbic acid, and phenols. These compounds have been found in the reaction media [36]. Subsequently, the “bottom-up” synthesis assisted with microwaves is carried out through the decomposition of these small organic molecules which can be converted into CQDs in four stages: (i) Condensation, such as amidation [37], aldo condensation [38] and Schiff base reactions [39]. These reactions depend on the disposition of ketone groups, aldehydes, esters, and amides (contained in powder orange peels; Fig. S3.1 and Table S3.3). (ii) Self-polymerization and carbonization. The carbon core grew especially at high temperature with the continuous carbonization and formed nanocristalline CQDs [40]. In this stage the heteroatom doping in the carbon core structure normally occurs [41] and in this case is facilitated by the powder orange peels highly enriched in minerals such as phosphorus, boron, aluminum, silicon, and sulfur (Table S3.4). (iii) Surface functionalization and/or passivation [29,42]. Finally, the residual pre-cursors such as carboxylic and amino groups can functionalize and/or can act as passivating agents capable of modifying the surface of CQDs to improve the photoluminescent characteristics and hydrophilic nature. Normally, CQDs are found in stable aqueous suspensions, this hydrophilic nature of carbonaceous nanomaterials is due to the surface chemistry of the particles. The most abundant functional groups in CQDs are the oxygenated such as –OH and –COOH, which come from of natural fiber compounds such as cellulose, hemicellulose, considering them as bioprecursors in the synthesis of CQDs. This property of hydrophilicity can be reversed by surface hydrolysis with other compounds to cause hydrophobicity of carbonaceous materials if for any reason flocculation and sedimentation of the CQD is required [43].

As mentioned above the formation of CQDs was likely to proceed via decomposition, self-polymerization, aggregation, carbonization, and surface functionalization/passivation [44]. The possible mechanism of formation was supported by HRTEM results as shown in Fig. 2 (b-e). The images were obtained from CQDs synthesized under the following reaction conditions: 220 °C for 30 min and 700 W. Fig. 2a shows the decomposition of cellulose, hemicellulose, ascorbic acid, glucose, sacarose, fructuose and amino acids from the powder of orange peels. The components polymerized into small dots, as the seeds to form the carbonaceous particles of organic materials namely “carbons nuclei” were formed due the condensation reactions. These carbon nuclei presented diameters of around 10–100 nm which consist of merely a few millions of carbon atoms [45]. The carbon nuclei came together to form long and branched polymeric chains, which presented an irregular shape that consists of a numerous nucleus of similar size (dp ≈ 38 nm) (Fig. 2b). It should be mentioned that a greater number of these carbonaceous polymer structures were observed in samples of CQDs suspensions synthesized at 180 °C for 30 min at 600 W and at 200 °C for 30 min and 650 W; this indicates that the formation of the CQDs was incomplete, and a large amount of the precursor remained in the polymerization phase (Fig. S3.2). Consecutively, the smallest and indivisible carbons nuclei gave rise to complex aggregates. These aggregates with a diameter of 10–30 nm were characterized as onion-like nanostructures (CNOs, _carbon nano-onios).

The diameter of onion-like nanostructures is controlled mainly by burning at high temperature [42]. Fig. 2c shows the aggregates of onion-like nanostructures which presented concentrically stacked carbon layers and whose diameter was approximately 25 nm. The distance between the parallel layers was constant and approximately equals 0.347 nm, similar to pure graphite [45]. The amorphous domains, situated in the central part of the CNOs were disordered mixtures of polycyclic, aromatic hydrocarbons and other organic and inorganic components [46]. Likewise, it is important to mention that both carbon polymeric structures and CNOs aggregates structures were identified in samples of CQDs solution synthesized under different conditions at 200 °C for 30 min and 650 W, which indicates that as the temperature and the reaction power increases, it was possible to appreciate a sequential formation of CQDs (Fig. S3.2). Consequently, nanocrystalline CQDs were formed in a fast process from concentric CNOs at higher temperature with the continuous carbonization (Fig. 2d–e). Towards the end of the reaction, the functionalization and passivation of the CQDs occurred, taking advantage of the rest of the organic precursors.

The aqueous dispersion of CQDs was analyzed by ICP-OES. Table S3.5 shows the concentration of elements such as phosphorus, boron, zinc, silice and arsenic which are present in the CQDs sample. These heteroatoms may be acting as doping agents in carbon core or the edge, which could promote a major conjugation of electronic transitions, a major tuning of electromagnetic absorption and emission phenomena [47] and photo-electrocatalytic behaviors from CQDs [48].

The optical properties of CQDs were characterized by UV–Visible spectroscopy. Fig. 3 shows the absorption spectrum with three absorption bands that exhibited features expected for core, edge and surface state transitions [49]. The first absorption band was observed at 364 nm which was attributed to a possible red shift associated to surface states on co-functionalized CQDs. These electronic transitions of the kind

H 2
and
− C = O
[50], and doping heteroatoms attached to the edge of the carbon core [51]. The broad band having maximum absorption at 284 nm is assigned to the edge transitions. This edge transition has been previously assigned to the
n −
π ∗
transition of the electrons from the nonbonding orbital of the surface functional groups [52] to the
π ∗
orbital of the sp2 carbon domain [53]. For example, the
n −
π ∗
transitions include the excitation of nonbonding electrons of O atoms in
− C = O
to the
π ∗
orbital of the hybridized C core [54].

Another band at 240 nm corresponds to
π −
π ∗
transitions of the conjugated system
C = C
within sp2 aromatic domains of the carbon core of CQDs [55], but also it is associated to the doping of the electron rich N atoms [56]. The co-functionalized CQDs were highly aqueous dispersible and emitted blue light under UV irradiation (360 nm), as shown in the insert picture of Fig. 3 compared with the CQDs suspension under daylight. Then, the optical absorption mechanism of CQDs mainly occurred in the ultraviolet regions, but with the surface functionalization of CQDs it is possible to promote the absorption at higher wavelength which clearly appeared around 364 nm in the spectrum, indicating a higher conjugation of electronic transitions between the diverse energy levels of CQDs particles. The results showed that functionalized-CQDs can be considered as sensitizers of semiconductor materials for the manufacture of photoelectrodes in solar cells.

Photoluminescence behavior is a property of CQDs considered for different applications. In this regard, Fig. 4 shows the fluorescence colors of co-functionalized CQDs obtained under optical microscopic of fluorescence. The microscope images (Fig. 4a–d) are presented. A wide range of emission colors ranging from blue to red were observed for an excitation wavelength sweep
λ
e x
from
365
t o
585
n m
. This phenomenon of tunable emission of multicolor can be associated to generic feature of graphitic carbon core [57], also on the basis of quantum confinement effect or the surface energy traps [56], to different surface defects [58], and due to the surface molecular states promoted by the presence of functional groups on the surface of the co-functionalized CQDs [59].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structure analysis of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The quantum confinement and crystalline structures properties depend on the diversity of raw materials and synthesis methods used for the preparation of CQDs [60]. The morphology of CQDs synthetized at 220 °C and 700 W for 30 min was analyzed using transmission electron microscopy of high resolution (HRTEM). Fig. 5 a shows the matrix with high graphitic constitution. These crystalline structures showed a lattice fringe corresponding to the planes of nanodiamond [61]. Nanodiamonds matrix can be converted to carbonaceous spherical structures like CNOs (Fig. 5b) with distances between the graphitic carbon sheets of 0.347 nm which is in a good accordance to the distance of the layers in bulk graphite [62]. At the same time, the CNOs displayed other concentric-spherical structures as CQDs by heat treatment until a total carbonization [63]. Consequently, Fig. 5c shows that CQDs were spherical and have uniform dispersion with not aggregation phenomenon [64].

The insert in Fig. 5c shows that CQDs have an obvious lattice fringe whose spacing was ≈ 0.22 nm and close to the graphite (100) crystal plane, which indicates that the CQDs have a degree of crystallinity [62]. Normally, diamond particles are transformed into spherical carbon dots at about 1700 °C, but also it is possible through microwave assisted methods of synthesis, where the conditions suggest a homogenous constant heating, pressure, and potential of irradiation on the starting materials [65]. Similarly, the CQDs samples synthesized under different conditions were analyzed by HRTEM: the first case at 180 °C and 600 W; and at 200 °C and 650 W for the second case. In this regard, the results showed that when the temperature and the power of microwave irradiation on the solution decreased, the complete CQDs formation was not achieved (Fig. S3.2). This indicates that the best conditions so far, for the CQDs synthesis, were 220 °C and 700 W for 30 min.

The CQDs were further examined by Raman spectroscopy and the results are shown in Fig. 5d. The spectrum showed the two characteristic D and G band, typical for carbon allotropes. These are attributed to the out-of-plane vibrations of the carbon atoms in the disordered structure or vacancy related to sp3 defects of CQDs and the in-plane vibrations of the graphitic sp2 carbon in the CQDs, respectively [66,67]. As show in Fig. 5d, the D and G bands of the CQDs produced by the carbonization step are observed at around 1393 and 1582 cm−1, respectively. The D band is related with possible surface defects such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, carbonyl and amino groups on the CQDs [66]. The G band is located at the characteristic value of crystalline graphite or graphene (1580 cm−1) [68], and became narrower in width with the increased intensity which again supports the improved crystallinity of the CQDs.

Fig. 5e shows the histogram plot of the size distribution of CQDs particles. Measurements were made in triplicate and the average diameter of the CQDs was confirmed to be 1.16 ± 0.1 nm for a homogeneous population of particles. These particles were obtained at 220 °C for 30 min and 700 W microwave irradiation. They always remained in aqueous suspension with a uniform dispersion. Also, two more samples that were synthesized at different reaction conditions were evaluated. Fig. S3.3 shows the histogram plots of the CQDs particle size distribution synthesized under different microwave power and temperature. It is observed that these parameters influenced the size of CQDs particles. The smallest CQDs particle size and its most uniform distribution were observed for those materials synthesized at 220 °C for 30 min and 700 W microwave irradiation (Fig. 5e). Conversely at a lower reaction temperature and at a lower power of microwave irradiation, QCDs particles with heterogeneous sizes were observed. Under reaction conditions at 200 °C and 650 W the obtained CQDs particles presented sizes between 15 and 2 nm. The material synthesized at 180 °C and 600 W presented a greater heterogeneity of the particle size whose sizes range from 2 to 40 nm (Fig. S3.3). However, this study confirmed that the synthesis procedure used promoted the formation of CQDs nanostructures with homogeneous size distribution with diameters between 1 and 2 nm. The particle nanometric size is very important because it has been observed that when the size of these carbon nanostructures was below 10 nm their optical properties of light absorption and fluorescence emission were better [69].

The zeta-potential measurements were performed in stationary state of CQDs nanoparticles. Fig. S3.4 shows that CQDs particles in suspension exhibited a net negative charge (−48.97 ± 1 mV) at pH 4.7 which was mainly attributed to the presence of carboxyl groups on the CQDs surface [70]. A decrease in the zeta potential value was observed in the suspension of CQDs materials synthesized to less temperature and power of reaction, −32.13 ± 1 mV at pH 4.5 and −23.06 ± 1 mV at pH 4.2, respectively (Fig. S3.4). The possible explanation of these results may be associated to the least amount of carboxyl groups on the surface of the CQDs, since at lower temperature and microwave power of reaction it is not possible to complete all the formation stages of the CQDs, especially the last two stages, that consisted of the total carbonization and functionalization/passivation (Fig. 2).

The orange peels contain organic compounds, mainly ascorbic acid, proteins, and polyphenols, just to mention a few organic compounds. These infer the presence of carboxylic acids and amine functional groups on the surface of the CQDs as recently reported. Fig. 6 shows the FT-IR spectrum of the prepared CQDs under reaction conditions at 200 °C, 30 min and microwave power of 700 W. The sharp bands located between 3741 and 2843 cm−1 are assigned to hydroxyl groups
O − H
stretching vibrations [71,72], which represent the huge number of outstanding phenolic-hydroxyl and carboxyl-hydroxyl groups on the surface of the CQDs [41],
C = C
of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons characteristic of CQDs [73] and
− C
H 2
and
− C
H 3
vibrations [74]. The band at 3494 cm−1 corresponds to stretching vibration of primary amino group (-NH2) or hydroxyl group (
O − H
). Whereas the band at 3207 cm−1 is related to
N − H
stretching vibration of amines or amides groups reported for CQDs doped with nitrogen [75]. This signal can be correlated with the absorption band observed at 364 nm in the UV–Vis spectrum (Fig. 3) which is attributed to the
n −
π ∗
transition within
C − N
H 2
. The vibration band observed at 1418 cm−1 is ascribed to carboxyl
− C = O
and
– C O O
vibration bending [76], which shows the presence of carboxylic group [52]. This peak confirms the result of UV–Vis absorbance spectrum at 284 nm attributed to
n −
π ∗
transitions of
− C = O
bonds of the carboxylic groups on CQDs (Fig. 3). Further, the band al 1373 cm−1 can be attributed to
C − N
H 2
stretching vibration of primary amines [77].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface charge distribution and pKa values of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The pHPZC is an important parameter related with the chemical surface of carbonaceous nanomaterials. It provides information about the degree of ionization on the material surface and its possible interactions with multiple molecules, compounds or ions. On the other hand, the determination of pHPZC brings with it the identification of certain functional groups on the surface of the CQDs based on the protonation and deprotonation mechanisms of the acid and basic surface functional groups. Additional protons from the solution can be either accepted or donated by the functional groups on the surface of the CQDs until reaching equilibrium. The surface of CQDs becomes positive when it accepts the protons (
− N
H +
e.g.) from the acidic solution, or negative if it loses protons (
− C O
O −
e.g.) to the basic solution [78].

Making use of the theoretical-experimental method reported by Jagiello [35] and Sederich [79], the proton biding curve, ion released (Q) vs. the pH, was obtained. Fig. 7 a shows that the release of protons from the CQDs surface was more marked towards a negative proton exchange related to the dissociation of acid functionalities such as carboxyl and phenolic groups [80]. The CQDs particles exhibited a pHpzc of 4.23, the value obtained from the proton biding curve. A computational procedure was applied on proton binding curves, Q(pH), to obtain the distribution function of pKa's. Fig. 7b shows the distribution function(pKa), f(pKa) for the presented species on the surface of CQDs. The two first ionization constants with pKa at 4.43 and 6.63 are associated to carboxylic groups [79]. The third peak at pKa 8.3 indicates the existence of phenolic groups [80]. These results agreed well those presented in FT-IR analysis.

An important use of UV–Visible light (contained in sunlight) can be absorbed and used to be transferred in photoelectrochemical systems or converted into energy through photovoltaic processes. In this sense, CQDs have potential application as new materials which act satisfactorily by absorbing UV–Visible, increasing the transfer of photoelectrons and reducing the speed of recombination of electron-hole pairs [26,81]. In such a way that considering the optical, chemical, and structural properties of the co-functionalized CQDs, these nanomaterials were combined with the TiO2 semiconductor material. In the resulting composite the CQDs were expected to act as photosensitizers of the semiconductor to promote an enhanced spectroscopic and photoelectrochemical performance when this composite (CQDs/TiO2) is used as hybrid material in the fabrication and application of photoanodes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the band-gap of CQDs/TiO2 composites
ce:section.ce:para:
Measurement of diffuse reflectance with a UV–visible spectrophotometer is a standard technique that in combination with the Kubelka-Munk-Tauc's [82] model it allows the determination of the absorption properties as the energy of the band-gap of a powder sample. This study was made to examine the influence of CQDs nanomaterial on the band-gap energy. Fig. 8 (a) shows the Tauc plots as function of band gap energy for TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 samples. The optical band gap E
g was estimated by extrapolating to photon energy axis a linear fit to plot of (khv)
1/2 against hv (often referred to as a Tauc plot). The commercial TiO2 known as P25 and synthetized mesoporous TiO2 samples present a large absorption coefficient for wavelengths at UV light with typical ban-gap energy between 3.07 and 2.98 eV, respectively. The best band gap value of 2.08 eV was presented by the hybrid material “CQDs (D
f
0)/TiO
2
”, where the TiO2 semiconductor is impregnated with concentrated CQDs. This fact can be associated with a proportion of visible spectrum absorbed by the modified semiconductor. The tuning of the energy levels is attributed to the presence of CQDs, and to the presence of heteroatoms such as nitrogen (Figs. 3 and 6) that were found as amino functional groups together with the influence of the carbonyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of CQDs (Figs. 3 and 6). The decrease of the band gap value depends of the amount of impregnated CQDs on TiO2, as it is listed in Table 1 .

Fig. 8 (b) shows the red-green-blue spectral composition (RGB) of the co-functionalized CQDs, which were determined from the profiles of fluorescence emission: 17% (R), 39% (G), 43% (B) respectively for a RGB of 90%. It has been observed that a high RGB parameter (>85%) is associated to the efficiency of conversion of electromagnetic radiation (UV and visible light) in electric energy [83]. The high RGB of the hybrid material “CQDs (D
f
0)/TiO
2
” can be attributed to the high electron density on the surface of the CQDs which reduces the energy band gap of the CQDs surface state transition, resulting in a red shift of the emission wavelength. This phenomenon consequently favors the decrease of the band gap of mesoporous TiO2 (Fig. 8a). Finally, once excited, generate the photo-excited electrons, which can be promoted to the conducting band of the TiO2, resulting in higher current density [26].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and composition analysis of the CQDs/TiO2 composites
ce:section.ce:para:
The surface morphology and elemental composition of “CQDs (D
f
0)/TiO
2
” was examined by SEM. Fig. 9 shows the SEM micrographs of CQDs-TiO2 samples, which were composed by spherical particles with several micrometer sizes. Fig. 9 (b) shows the EDAX analysis which revealed the presence of titanium, oxygen, carbon and nitrogen in the CQDs/TiO2 hybrid material. The elemental distribution of CQDs/TiO2 was examined by SEM/EDS elemental color mapping analysis. The hybrid material contains Ti, O, C, and N (Fig. 9 c-f). The presence of nitrogen and certain proportion of oxygen can be attributed to the surface functionalization of the CQDs nanomaterials associated to the carboxyl and amine groups. The strong coupling between elements allows a synergistic interaction, which could contribute to a better optical performance and load transfer by the hybrid material.

On the other hand, the crystalline nature of as-sensibilized “CQDs(D
f
0)/TiO
2
” was further analyzed by the XRD patterns, which are shown in Fig. 10 . The CQDs presented a remarkable diffraction peak at 2θ = 25.2°, illustrating their crystallinity structure, which was corroborated by diffractograms of the same material but varying the concentration of CQDs in the composite CQDs/TiO2. The diffraction signal corresponding to the crystallographic structure of the CQDs shows a decrease in the intensity of the peak with respect to the decrease of their concentration in the hybrid materials (Fig. S3.5). Also, others major diffraction peaks of the CQDs/TiO2 were found at 2θ = 26.5°, 37.7°, 48.1°, 51.4°, 55.1°, and 62.7°corresponding to the (101), (004), (200), (211) y (118) crystallographic planes of TiO2 anatase phase. The diffraction pattern matches with the standard Card of TiO2 anatase phase [34,84]. Also, there were small peaks at 2θ = 34.7° 69° and 70° which correspond to TiO2 rutile planes. The percentage of crystalline phases contained in the materials CQDs/TiO2 was 98.3% and 1.7% of anatase and rutile, respectively. The importance of having a higher percentage of anatase favors the absorption efficiency of the incident light since the rutile phase only absorbs 4%, so that higher anatase content will contribute effectively to the capture and transport of induced photoelectrons from CQDs sensitized semiconductor, which favors their possible application in the manufacture of photoelectrodes [85].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoelectrochemical characterization of the CQDs/TiO2 thin films
ce:section.ce:para:
In accordance with their excellent optical properties, the CQDs were used as photosensitizer of TiO2 thin film electrodes to further explore their photoelectrocatalytic activity and their potential application as CQDs/TiO2 photoanode in solar cells. First, studies of the electrochemical and photoelectrochemical properties were carried out using [“CQDs(D
f
0)/TiO
2
”]/FTO as working photoelectrodes. Fig. 11 (a) shows the cyclic voltammograms of bare FTO (curve i), TiO2/FTO (curve ii) and [“CQDs(D
f
0)/TiO
2
”]/FTO (curve iii) in 0.1 M KCl as supporting electrolyte. These curves showed an independent photoelectrochemical behavior of each electrode under UV–Visible irradiation. The voltametric response (curve i) presented only a cathodic peak at Epc = −0.60 V, which is attributed to the reduction of the FTO material itself. The TiO2/FTO electrode revealed an oxidation and reduction process at Epa 1.11 V and a more defined cathodic peak at Epc −0.50 V [86], respectively. Finally, the CQDs/TiO2/FTO electrode presented an enhance of oxidation process at Epa −0.260 V, which can be particularly associated with the oxidation of the oxygenated functional groups, such as carboxyl, on the surface of the CQDs. These functional groups are deprotonated at pH 5.2 of the electrolytic solution (Fig. 7) and electrochemically they can be oxidized. The reduction peak at Epc −0.44 V may involve the electroreduction of the same carboxyl functional groups, but possibly, also the reduction of TiO2. Comparing the photoelectrochemical behavior on each electrode, we can highlight that the [“CQDs(D
f
0)/TiO
2
”]/FTO electrode showed a better and higher photocatalytic performance. This fact occurs due to the action of “CQDs” as photosensitizers of the TiO2 material, capable of absorbing and taking advantage of the electromagnetic irradiation of UV–Visible light (characteristic of practically all types of CQDs [87–89]), in addition to act as a light harvester and electron reservoir into the composite material to promote a higher density and accumulation of photoelectrons available at the interface to be transferred within the electrochemical system [87]. The photo-electrocatalytic performance is also favored by the presence of heteroatoms (N, B, Si, P) in the structure of the CQDs (Table S3.5), which are used as electron deficient and electron rich dopant models [41] capable of increasing the absorption coefficient of UV–Vis light [90–92]. Resulting in rapidly quenched holes and a higher concentration of long-lived photogenerated electrons [49] that led to increase in the photocurrent flow, which was three times higher in the photoelectrode “CQDs(D
f
0)/TiO
2
”/FTO due to the presence of CQDs in the composite material.

The greater absorption and use of photons to be transferred as electric current, may be associated with the electrochemical band gap of the different materials that make up the manufactured photoelectrodes. The cyclic voltammetry (CV) methods were used to measure the energy levels of molecular orbitals of the structures that make up the different modified electrodes. The CV curves were performed in 0.1 M KCl at a potential scan rate of 25 mV s−1. The scan started at the open circuit potential in a cathodic direction until a reversal potential of Eλ = −0.8 V, then in anodic direction until a reversal potential of Eλ = 1.2 V vs. Ag/AgCl/NaCl 3 M. The LUMO and HOMO levels were obtained arising from the following equations: ELUMO = -e(Ered + 4.4) V, EHOMO = -e(Eox + 4.4) V [29]. Fig. 11(b) shows the graph that indicates the energy values in eV of the HOMO and LUMO levels both for the FTO substrate material and for the electrodes: TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2. The classification: “CQDs(D
f
0)/TiO
2
”/FTO, “CQDs(D
f
1)/TiO
2
”/FTO, “CQDs(D
f
2)/TiO
2
”/FTO was assigned by the variation in the concentration of the carbonaceous nanomaterial as a photosensitizer. These results corroborate that the presence of CQDs in the thin film composition favors the decrease in the electrochemical band gap energy when the concentration of CQDs increases in the hybrid material. The sequence of the band gap energy values in each case was: FTO = 2.3 eV, TiO2 = 1.9 eV, CQDs (Df 2) = 1.6 eV, CQDs (Df 1) = 1.45 eV, CQDs (Df 0) = 0.9 eV. This is attributed to the graphitic core and the abundant number of oxygenated groups on the surface of the CQDs. The new alignment of the Fermi levels between the materials of the photoelectrodes “CQDs(D
f
0)/TiO
2
”/FTO allows the best performance in terms of charge transfer kinetics in the electrochemical system.

Consecutively, the photoelectrochemical tests of the “CQDs(D
f
0)/TiO
2
”/FTO electrodes was made under dark and irradiation of both UV and UV–Visible light. Fig. 11(c) show the CVs responses under dark (curve i), UV light (curve ii), UV–Visible light (curve iii) respectively. Notably, it was observed that the major photocatalytic activity of “CQDs(D
f
0)/TiO
2
” thin-film material happened when the UV–Visible light irradiation was applied. This photocatalytic behavior is associate to the PEC efficiency with a photocurrent flux density value (J = 1.15 mA cm−2) which is significantly higher than that reported as photoresponse (J less than 0.5 mA cm−2) by Wang et al. for photoanodes of TiO2/(Y,R)-CQDs [93]. These results were complemented with electrochemical impedance spectroscopy measurements (Fig. S3.6) shows that the CQDs/TiO2/FTO electrode performed better when applying UV–Visible light, since it presented the lower electrical resistivity associated with its lower value of charge transfer resistance (Rct). This implies a speed-up electron transfer at the interface thanks to the increase in the photoelectroactive area generated by the thin film of the “CQDs(D
f
0)/TiO
2
” composite material.

The photoelectroactive surface area of “CQDs(D
f
0)/TiO
2
”/FTO photoelectrode was determined under conditions of dark and illumination whit light by carrying out cyclic voltammograms of 5 mM K4[Fe(CN)6] in 0.1 M KCl at a scan rate from 20 to 160 mV s−1. The estimation of the values of the photoelectroactive surface areas were calculated using the Randles-Sevcik equation (Ec. S1). The thin film “CQDs(D
f
0)/TiO
2
” electrode presented a higher photoelectroactive surface area when it was illuminated with UV–Vis light (Fig. 11d) because the composite “CQDs(D
f
0)/TiO
2
” promoted a higher concentration of photogenerated electrons, which in turn were delocalized and transferred rapidly within the thin film to produce electric current. In the same sense, the rate of charge transfer was increased when the photoelectrode was irradiated with UV–Vis light because of the increase in the electroactive surface area. This phenomenon can be corroborated by evaluating the kinetics of the photoelectrochemical system. In this case the value of the constant of the rate of charge transfer promoted by the “CQDs(D
f
0)/TiO
2
”/FTO photoelectrode in the electrochemical system was determined, performing cyclic voltammetry from 20 to 120 mV s−1 and using the K4[Fe(CN)6] redox probe. The value of the photocharge transfer constant (kpct) was calculated using the equation (Ec. S2). Through this study it was corroborated (Fig. 11d) that the higher value of photocharge transfer constant (kpct) was also dependent on irradiation with UV–Vis light and favored over the phototelectrode under this condition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of CQDs/TiO2 photoelectrodes in CQDSSC
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 12 shows the current-voltage curves obtained for the different carbon quantum dots-sensitized solar cells (CQDSSC) prepared from the different CQD/TiO2 materials obtained and characterized in previous sections. Typically, in this kind of devices, QD absorbed photons and the electrons in the valence band (VB) are promoted at the conduction band (CB), generating electrons/holes pairs. The electrons are quickly transferred into CB of a metal oxide (in this case TiO2). These charges carriers are driven through of mesoporous TiO2 layer through mesoporous TiO2 layer to FTO electrode and then get the counter electrode. Finally, the system is kept in equilibrium thanks to the redox couple species [94]. As see in Fig. 12 all CQDSSC tested presented the characteristic I–V curve shape of a photovoltaic device; from this figure and Table 2 , it is observed that the presence of the CQDs enhanced the performance of the CQDSSC. The values of the photovoltaic parameter of the CQDSSC increased with the increase concentration of CQDs. The CQDSSC with the higher amount of CQDs presented the highest conversion efficiency of 0.184%. It is worth mentioning that although these efficiency results were relatively low values, the materials showed relevant photovoltaic properties, since the material proved to be photosensitive and capable of generating photocurrent (see Fig. 11). It would be possible to explore different studies in future works with the aim of improving the performance of these solar cells such as electron/hole pair recombination inside CQD/TiO2 photoanode on their surface due to imperfect deposition and fabrication, losses due to poor charge transport at the CQD/TiO2-electrolyte interface or phenomena of high resistance in the different QDs/TiO2-electrolyte-counter electrode due to possible redox reactions [95].

Table 3 lists the photovoltaic parameters obtained from previous studies and current work for comparation. In this study the optoelectronic device showed important photovoltaic values in solar cell which can be competitive with the exemplified devices, mainly with those who have been sensitized with CQDs synthesized from biomass [29] and environmentally friendly processes. These photovoltaic parameters can be improved by studying the influence of the concentration of CQDs and the functionalization of the CQDs/TiO2 composites, which are being carried out.

In this study co-functionalized CQDs were synthesized through a one-step, low-cost and environmentally friendly microwave solvothermal method, using the powder of orange peels as a biomass source. The reaction parameters, such as temperature, time and microwave irradiation power had an important influence on the formation mechanism of CQDs nanomaterials, their graphitic crystalline structure and surface co-functionalization. The optimized reaction parameters were determined. In addition to oxygen, the materials presented other doping elements such as N, B, Si, P and Si, which were beneficial to improve the optical properties due to the decreasing of the optical and photoelectrochemical band gap of a typical TiO2 semiconductor when they formed CQDs/TiO2 composites. These composites enhanced the UV–Vis light absorption capacity of the CQDs/TiO2 composites to generate the photo-excited electrons, promoting the electron-transfer to the conducting band of the TiO2 resulting in higher flow of current density. Taking advantage of this property the CQDs/TiO2 composites were evaluated as photoanodes in CQDSSC showing higher conversion of photonic into electrical energy than in previous works.
